spec.description:
Remaining capacity before commits start waiting. 

- Flatline at 0: commits are stalling now.
spec.title: Permits Available

spec.description:
Left axis - how many commits had to wait for the background persist thread in the last 5 minutes. 

Right axis: p99 commit latency how long a slow commit actually took.

Pairs frequency with duration for example 3000 stalls at 0.1ms is fine; 10 stalls at 500ms is not.
spec.title: Commit Stall - Frequency and Duration

spec.description:
Breaks block processing into three pieces:
1. EVM Execution: compute time with trie read time subtracted out
2. Trie Ops: reads, updates, and Merkle hashing combined
3. Block Write: time in the write path that is not accounted for by sub timers

The sum line (D) is total time. When it rises, look at which piece grew.
spec.title: EVM vs Trie vs Write

spec.description:
Firewood write path measured inside Rust, with no Go/CGO overhead.

- Propose >> Commit: CPU bound (hashing is the bottleneck)
- Commit >> Propose: I/O bound (disk writes are the bottleneck)
spec.title: Firewood Write Path: Propose vs Commit

spec.description:
Average gas consumed per transaction.

Higher values mean complex contract interactions. Lower values mean simple transfers. Useful for understanding why some block ranges are slower than others.

- ~21,000 gas: simple ETH transfers
- 50,000 to 100,000: token transfers (ERC 20)
- 100,000 to 500,000: complex DeFi (swaps, liquidity)
Above 500,000: heavy computation
spec.title: Transaction Complexity

spec.description:
Ratio of storage reads to account reads. Tells you what kind of workload is running.

- Above 10: heavy contract state, deep storage tries
- 3 to 10: contract heavy
- 1 to 3: mixed workload
Below 1: account heavy, mostly simple transfers
spec.title: State Access Pattern

spec.description:
Average disk read latency: total disk read time divided by number of disk reads.

This only counts cache misses, so it reflects the actual storage I/O cost per read.
spec.title: Disk Read Latency

spec.description: Trie mutation rates broken down by type.
spec.title: Trie Mutations: Insert & Remove

spec.description: Percentage of allocations coming from the free list (reused) versus new file space (from_end), broken down by size class.
spec.title: Allocator Reuse Efficiency

spec.description: Overall throughput: gas processed per second across the chain.
spec.title: Gas Processed

spec.description:
Overlays Go side EVM read time with Firewood disk I/O time.

Go: account_reads + storage_reads. Total time the EVM waits on state reads, accumulated across every individual account and storage lookup in the block. This includes reads that hit cache and reads that miss.

Firewood disk: io.read_ms. Only the time spent on actual disk reads, meaning cache misses that went to storage.

A wide gap means most read time is spent on things other than disk. When the lines get close, disk I/O is dominating read cost.
spec.title: State Reads: Go vs Firewood Disk

spec.description:
Shows where time goes inside a Firewood commit, from outermost layer to innermost.

- Go: Commit: Full envelope — mutex, CGO crossing, Rust work, root hash verify, Go cleanup.
- Rust: Commit: Pure Rust time inside CGO.
- Disk I/O: flush_nodes — actual background disk writes.
- Disk I/O Efficiency: flush_nodes / ffi_commit_ms — what fraction of commit time is disk.

Reading the gaps:
- Go: Commit minus Rust: Commit = Go/CGO overhead (mutex, CGO crossing, root verify, cleanup)
- Rust: Commit minus Disk I/O = Rust non-disk overhead (revision reaping, proposal cleanup, header writes)
- Disk I/O / Rust: Commit = I/O Efficiency (right axis)

When all three lines converge and efficiency is near 100%: disk dominates the entire commit stack.

When efficiency drops below 75%: non-disk Rust overhead
spec.title: Commit Stack

spec.description:
Node cache hit rates for read and write paths.

Higher read cache hit rate means fewer disk reads.
spec.title: Node Cache Health

spec.description:
How often the allocator finds the next free slot in cache versus walking the on disk linked list.

Above 0.90 is healthy; the allocator rarely has to hit disk.
If it drops, the cache is either too small or allocation churn is too high. Each miss costs a disk read to walk the free list.
spec.title: Freelist Cache Hit Rate

spec.description: Commit latency percentiles from histogram buckets: p50 (typical), p95 (slow), p99 (tail).
spec.title: Commit Latency Distribution (p50 / p95 / p99)

spec.description: Propose latency percentiles from histogram buckets: p50 (typical), p95 (slow), p99 (tail).
spec.title: Propose Latency Distribution (p50 / p95 / p99)

spec.description:
Subtracts Rust timing from Go wrapper timing for both propose and commit.

Propose overhead = triedb/hash/time minus ffi.propose_ms
Commit overhead = triedb/commit/time minus ffi.commit_ms

The propose gap includes: mutex acquisition, building the batch from Go data structures (iterating dirty accounts/storage to prepare the FFI call), CGO crossing, and Go side cleanup. Batch creation can be a real chunk of work, not just crossing overhead.

The commit gap includes: mutex acquisition, proposal lookup, CGO crossing, root hash verification after the FFI returns, and Go side proposal cleanup.

Near zero means Go adds negligible time on top of Rust.
Spikes usually point to mutex contention or Go GC pauses.
Consistently nonzero is normal if batch creation or proposal lookup is doing real work.
spec.title: Go/CGO Overhead: Propose & Commit

spec.description:
Proposal queue depth and revision utilization.

Uncommitted Proposals (gauge): how many proposals are waiting to be committed. If this climbs, commits cannot keep up with proposals. That is backpressure.
Active Revisions (gauge): how many historical revisions are in memory.
Max Revisions (gauge): the configured limit. When active gets close to max, oldest revisions get aggressively reaped.

Healthy state: uncommitted stays at 0 to 2, active well below max.
Danger: uncommitted climbing means commit backpressure. Active equals max means constant reaping.
spec.title: Proposal Queue & Revision Pressure

spec.description:
Commit and propose error rates.

The #[metrics] proc macro tags every commit and propose with success=true or success=false. This panel shows the failure rate.

Commit failures typically mean the proposal parent was not the latest revision (a concurrent commit race) or there was a disk I/O error.
Propose failures mean a merkle operation error or a node store allocation failure.

Any non zero value here needs investigation.
spec.title: Error & Discard Rates

spec.description:
What fraction of commit time is actual disk I/O?

Calculated as flush_nodes divided by ffi.commit_ms.

Near 100%: commit is I/O bound, disk is the bottleneck. Faster disks or storage optimization would help.
Low (say 30%): most commit time is overhead from revision reaping, root store, or proposal cleanup. Worth digging into why.

Use alongside the Commit Stack panel to see the absolute numbers.
spec.title: Commit I/O Efficiency

spec.description: Where node reads come from: file (disk) vs memory (in flight proposals).
spec.title: Node Read Source (Disk vs In-Memory Proposals)

spec.description: How many disk I/O calls happen per logical node read.
spec.title: Read Amplification (Physical I/O per Node Read)

spec.description: Batch processing latency percentiles: p50, p95, p99.
spec.title: Batch Latency Distribution (p50 / p95 / p99)

spec.description: What sizes of nodes are being deleted.
spec.title: Node Deletion by Area Size

spec.description: Proposal creation and lifecycle patterns.
spec.title: Proposal Lifecycle

spec.description: How much space comes from reuse (free lists) vs new allocation (end of file).
spec.title: Allocator Space Flow (Reuse vs Growth)
